gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Role of external state and non-state actors in creating challenges to internal security.
prelimsPoints: An Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) pilot project launched in the Northeast.
IBMS uses technologies like drones, thermal imagers, and ground sensors.
Aims to curb illegal infiltration, smuggling, and cross-border crimes.
Ministry of Home Affairs is the nodal ministry.
The project seeks to enhance real-time monitoring and coordination among border forces.
mainsPoints: Discuss the need for an Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) in India, particularly in challenging terrains like the Northeast.
Analyze how technological advancements can enhance border security and address various internal security challenges.
Evaluate the effectiveness of IBMS in curbing illegal cross-border activities and improving the coordination among border guarding forces.
Examine the challenges in implementing and scaling up such advanced systems across India's diverse border regions.
GS3.imageDescription: A border post with surveillance cameras and drones flying overhead.
GS3.trivia: India has a total land border of over 15,000 kilometers, shared with seven countries.

GS3.headline: Government Introduces New Scheme to Promote Organic Farming and Sustainable Agriculture
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare has launched a new national scheme, 'Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana Plus' (PKVY+), to aggressively promote organic and sustainable farming practices.
• The scheme offers financial incentives to farmers for adopting organic certification, soil health management, and integrated pest management techniques.
• PKVY+ aims to create Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) focused on organic produce, facilitating market linkages and value addition for farmers.
• Emphasis is placed on reducing chemical fertilizer and pesticide use, enhancing soil biodiversity, and promoting climate-resilient agricultural systems.
• The initiative aligns with India's commitments to sustainable development goals and enhancing farmers' income through eco-friendly methods.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System—objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
Food processing and related industries in India—scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
prelimsPoints: PKVY+ (Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana Plus) promotes organic and sustainable farming.
It offers financial incentives for organic certification and soil health management.
Focus on creating Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) for organic produce.
Aims to reduce chemical fertilizer use and enhance soil biodiversity.
Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare is the nodal ministry.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of promoting organic farming and sustainable agriculture in India for food security, environmental health, and farmers' income.
Analyze the features and potential impact of schemes like PKVY+ in encouraging a shift towards eco-friendly farming practices.
Evaluate the role of Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) in strengthening the value chain for organic produce and empowering farmers.
Examine the challenges faced by organic farmers in India and suggest policy measures to overcome them.
GS3.imageDescription: A farmer tending to an organic vegetable field.
GS3.trivia: Sikkim was declared India's first fully organic state in 2016.

GS3.headline: India Develops Indigenous mRNA Vaccine for Emerging Viral Threat
GS3.explainer:
• Indian scientists have successfully developed and fast-tracked an indigenous mRNA vaccine targeting a newly identified viral strain with pandemic potential.
• The vaccine, developed through a public-private partnership, utilizes cutting-edge mRNA technology, allowing for rapid development and scalability.
• This achievement marks a significant milestone in India's journey towards self-reliance in vaccine development and public health preparedness.
• Clinical trials have shown promising results in terms of safety and efficacy, paving the way for emergency use authorization.
• The development strengthens India's 'Vaccine Maitri' initiative, positioning the country as a global contributor to health security.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Biotechnology.
prelimsPoints: India developed an indigenous mRNA vaccine for a new viral strain.
mRNA technology allows for rapid vaccine development.
This is a significant step towards self-reliance in vaccine development.
Clinical trials showed promising safety and efficacy.
Contributes to India's 'Vaccine Maitri' initiative.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of indigenous mRNA vaccine development for India's public health security and global health leadership.
Analyze the advantages of mRNA vaccine technology over traditional vaccine platforms, especially in responding to emerging viral threats.
Evaluate the role of public-private partnerships and government support in accelerating vaccine research and development in India.
Examine how India's 'Vaccine Maitri' initiative contributes to global health equity and strengthens its soft power.
GS3.imageDescription: A scientist in a lab coat holding a vial of vaccine.
GS3.trivia: mRNA vaccines work by teaching our cells how to make a protein—or even just a piece of a protein—that triggers an immune response inside our bodies.

GS3.headline: Smart Cities Mission Phase II Launched with Focus on Green Infrastructure
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs has officially launched Phase II of the Smart Cities Mission, with an enhanced focus on green infrastructure and climate-resilient urban planning.
• This new phase targets 100 additional cities, emphasizing sustainable mobility, renewable energy integration, water management, and urban biodiversity conservation.
• Phase II aims to build upon the successes and lessons learned from the initial phase, integrating advanced digital solutions for efficient municipal services and citizen engagement.
• Increased funding allocations and incentives for innovative green technologies are central to achieving the mission's objectives.
• The initiative seeks to transform Indian cities into more livable, sustainable, and economically vibrant hubs, addressing rapid urbanization challenges.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission Phase II launched, focusing on green infrastructure.
Targets 100 additional cities.
Emphasizes sustainable mobility, renewable energy, and water management.
Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs is the nodal ministry.
Aims to make cities more livable and sustainable.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and objectives of India's Smart Cities Mission, highlighting the enhanced focus on green infrastructure in Phase II.
Analyze how sustainable urban planning and the integration of digital solutions can address the challenges of rapid urbanization in India.
Evaluate the potential impact of Smart Cities Mission Phase II on environmental sustainability, economic growth, and quality of life in Indian cities.
Examine the role of citizen participation and innovative technologies in the successful implementation of smart city initiatives.
GS3.imageDescription: A futuristic city skyline with green spaces and smart technology elements.
GS3.trivia: The Smart Cities Mission was launched by Prime Minister Narendra Modi in 2015, initially selecting 100 cities.

GS3.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Exceeds Projections, Driven by Manufacturing and Services
GS3.explainer:
• India's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) registered an impressive growth rate of 7.8% in the second quarter (July-September) of 2025, surpassing most expert predictions.
• The robust performance was primarily fueled by strong growth in the manufacturing sector, buoyed by increased industrial output and government investment in infrastructure.
• The services sector, particularly IT, financial services, and hospitality, also contributed significantly to the upward trajectory, indicating a broad-based economic recovery.
• Private consumption showed resilience, supported by improving consumer sentiment and festival demand, while investment picked up pace.
• The positive indicators reinforce India's position as one of the fastest-growing major economies globally, despite prevailing global economic uncertainties.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
Investment models.
GS3.source: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation
prelimsPoints: India's Q2 (July-September 2025) GDP grew by 7.8%.
Growth primarily driven by manufacturing and services sectors.
Private consumption also showed resilience.
Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation releases GDP data.
India remains one of the fastest-growing major economies.
mainsPoints: Analyze the key drivers behind India's robust GDP growth in Q2 2025 and its implications for the overall economic outlook.
Discuss the role of government policies, investment, and private consumption in sustaining economic momentum in India.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India to maintain high growth rates amidst global economic uncertainties and geopolitical shifts.
Examine the sectoral contributions to GDP growth and the measures needed to ensure inclusive and sustainable development.
GS3.imageDescription: A graph showing upward trend of economic growth.
GS3.trivia: GDP measures the monetary value of all finished goods and services made within a country during a specific period.

sources: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation
